perturbation.original_text: 3. Injunctive Relief. Member acknowledges that any failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement will cause Franchisor irreparable injury, and Member agrees to pay all court costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred by Franchisor in obtaining specific performance of, or an injunction against violation of, the requirements of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Delay. Member acknowledges that any failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement will cause Franchisor irreparable injury, and Member agrees to pay all court costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred by Franchisor in obtaining specific performance of, or an injunction against violation of, the requirements of this Agreement. No delay or failure by the Franchisor or Franchisee to exercise any right under this Agreement, and no partial or single exercise of that right, shall constitute a waiver of that or any other right provided herein, and no waiver of any violation of any terms and provisions of this Agreement shall be construed as a waiver of any succeeding violation of the same or any other provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the injunctive relief clause to the section about delays buries a critical remedy for the franchisor. Injunctive relief is usually placed near dispute resolution or remedies sections to highlight its importance and immediate availability. Placing it under 'Delay' obscures its enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 65 dictates the procedures for obtaining injunctive relief. By obscuring the explicit acknowledgment of irreparable harm and the agreement to court costs within a section on delays, the contract makes it harder to quickly and easily demonstrate the requirements for an injunction, potentially hindering the franchisor's ability to obtain immediate relief from a court.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a legal contradiction by obscuring the injunctive relief clause, which is critical for the franchisor's ability to obtain immediate relief, as detailed in the perturbation and law explanations. While the changed text does not explicitly remove the possibility of injunctive relief, it buries the clause within a section about delays, making it less prominent and potentially hindering its enforceability. FRCP 65, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, outlines procedures for obtaining injunctive relief, emphasizing the need for demonstrating immediate and irreparable injury. By moving the acknowledgment of irreparable harm and agreement to court costs to the 'Delay' section, the contract weakens the franchisor's position to quickly and easily meet these requirements, thus contradicting the principle of immediate relief that injunctive remedies are designed to provide.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Covenants Not to Compete. (a) Member specifically acknowledges that, pursuant to the Franchise Agreement, and by virtue of its position with Franchisee, Member will receive valuable specialized training and Confidential Information, including, without limitation, information regarding the operational, sales, promotional, and marketing methods and techniques of Franchisor and the System. (b) Member covenants and agrees that during the term of the Franchise Agreement, except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member shall not, either directly or indirectly, for itself, or through, on behalf of, or in conjunction with any person, persons, partnership, corporation, or entity: (i) Own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, rent or lease to, or have any other interest in business that (directly or indirectly) operates, or grants franchises or licenses to operate, a restaurant featuring pizza and related food specialties or that offers products or services substantially similar to those then offered by Pizza Fusions Restaurants ("Competitive Business"); C - 1 Source: PF HOSPITALITY GROUP INC., 10-12G, 9/23/2015 (ii) Divert or attempt to divert any business or customer, or potential business or customer, to any Competitive Business; or (iii) Induce any person to leave his or her employment with Franchisee or Franchisor. (c) Member covenants and agrees that during the Post-Term Period (defined below), except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member shall not, either directly or indirectly, own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, within a three (3) mile radius of the premises of your Franchised Business or within a three (3) mile radius of any Franchised Business then-operating or under construction to operate under the System. (d) As used in this Agreement, the term "Post-Term Period" shall mean a continuous uninterrupted period of two (2) years from the date of: (a) a transfer permitted under Section 14 of the Franchise Agreement; (b) expiration or termination of the Franchise Agreement (regardless of the cause for termination); (c) termination of Member's employment with Franchisee; and/or (d) a final order of a duly authorized arbitrator, panel of arbitrators, or a court of competent jurisdiction (after all appeals have been taken) with respect to any of the foregoing or with respect to the enforcement of this Agreement; either directly or indirectly (through, on behalf of, or in conjunction with any persons, partnership, corporation or entity).
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Covenants Not to Compete. (a) Member specifically acknowledges that, pursuant to the Franchise Agreement, and by virtue of its position with Franchisee, Member will receive valuable specialized training and Confidential Information, including, without limitation, information regarding the operational, sales, promotional, and marketing methods and techniques of Franchisor and the System. (b) Member covenants and agrees that during the term of the Franchise Agreement, except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member shall not, either directly or indirectly, for itself, or through, on behalf of, or in conjunction with any person, persons, partnership, corporation, or entity: (i) Own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, rent or lease to, or have any other interest in business that (directly or indirectly) operates, or grants franchises or licenses to operate, a restaurant featuring pizza and related food specialties or that offers products or services substantially similar to those then offered by Pizza Fusions Restaurants ("Competitive Business"); (ii) Divert or attempt to divert any business or customer, or potential business or customer, to any Competitive Business; or (iii) Induce any person to leave his or her employment with Franchisee or Franchisor. 6. Third-Party Beneficiary. Member covenants and agrees that during the Post-Term Period (defined below), except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member shall not, either directly or indirectly, own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, within a three (3) mile radius of the premises of your Franchised Business or within a three (3) mile radius of any Franchised Business then-operating or under construction to operate under the System. (d) As used in this Agreement, the term "Post-Term Period" shall mean a continuous uninterrupted period of two (2) years from the date of: (a) a transfer permitted under Section 14 of the Franchise Agreement; (b) expiration or termination of the Franchise Agreement (regardless of the cause for termination); (c) termination of Member's employment with Franchisee; and/or (d) a final order of a duly authorized arbitrator, panel of arbitrators, or a court of competent jurisdiction (after all appeals have been taken) with respect to any of the foregoing or with respect to the enforcement of this Agreement; either directly or indirectly (through, on behalf of, or in conjunction with any persons, partnership, corporation or entity). Member hereby acknowledges and agrees that Franchisor is an intended third-party beneficiary of this Agreement with the right to enforce it, independently or jointly with Franchisee.
perturbation.explanation: Moving subsection 'c' of Section 2 regarding post-term covenants to Section 6, which is about Third-Party Beneficiary, significantly reduces clarity regarding non-compete obligations after the agreement ends. Typically, the entire scope of non-compete clauses is provided in a single section, ensuring the franchisee understands all limitations at once. By burying part of the covenant in a completely unrelated section, it makes it more difficult for the member to understand the extent of their post-term obligations, and this weakens the covenant's enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 188 deals with restraints of trade and requires that covenants not to compete be reasonable. By splitting the non-compete clause into multiple sections, the altered contract makes it more difficult for a court to assess the reasonableness of the entire restraint, as a key component (the post-term restrictions) is not readily apparent within the main clause outlining non-compete obligations. This can lead to a finding of unreasonableness and unenforceability.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation involves a structural flaw by moving the post-term non-compete obligations to a section concerning third-party beneficiaries. The Law Explanation suggests that by splitting the non-compete clause, the contract makes it harder for a court to assess the reasonableness of the restraint. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction,' where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section, making it difficult for parties to interpret, ultimately weakening enforceability. Although the scraped snippets do not directly refer to laws about the structure of non-compete clauses, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation together suggest that moving part of the covenant to another section makes it more likely for it to be unenforceable due to lack of clarity.
